sections.title: Phrasal verbs
items: **cross out** — draw a line through sth written
**look up** — try to find information in a book, etc
**read out** — say sth out loud which you are reading
**turn over** — turn sth so the other side is towards you
**write down** — write information on a piece of paper

exercises.title: Topic vocabulary (gap fill)
exercises.instructions: Complete using the correct form of the words and phrases given.
exercises.studentInstruction: Tap the correct word or phrase.

items.sentence: Maria studied hard and hopes she will ...... (achieve / fail / pass) the exam.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: After studying hard, Maria hopes to pass the exam.

wrongOptions.achieve / fail / pass: The bare infinitive (achieve / fail / pass) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Choose the future form that matches whether the sentence predicts, plans, or arranges.

items.sentence: If you don't revise, you might ...... (achieve / fail / pass) the test.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Without revision you might fail the test.

wrongOptions.achieve / fail / pass: The bare infinitive (achieve / fail / pass) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Modals and semi-modals express ability, obligation, permission, or possibility — match the nuance in the sentence.

items.sentence: Our class has ...... (achieve / fail / pass) excellent results this term.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is results this term, so the correct answer is "achieved".

wrongOptions.achieve / fail / pass: The bare infinitive (achieve / fail / pass) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple misses the link to now — present perfect needs have/has + past participle.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect (have/has + past participle) links past actions to now or unfinished time.

items.sentence: Please read the ...... (degree / experience / instruction) before you start the activity.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Read the instructions before starting an activity.

wrongOptions.degree / experience / instruction: The bare infinitive (degree / experience / instruction) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Conjugate (degree / experience / instruction) to match the subject and the time clues in the sentence.

items.sentence: Working in a school was a useful ...... (degree / experience / instruction) for Liam.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Working in a school was useful experience.

wrongOptions.degree / experience / instruction: The bare infinitive (degree / experience / instruction) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Time words like already, yet, since, and for are strong signals for present perfect.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Conjugate (degree / experience / instruction) to match the subject and the time clues in the sentence.

items.sentence: You need a university ...... (degree / experience / instruction) for this job.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: This job needs a university degree.

wrongOptions.degree / experience / instruction: The bare infinitive (degree / experience / instruction) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Time words like already, yet, since, and for are strong signals for present perfect.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Conjugate (degree / experience / instruction) to match the subject and the time clues in the sentence.

items.sentence: Typing quickly is an important ...... (course / qualification / skill).

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Typing quickly is a practical skill.

wrongOptions.course / qualification / skill: The bare infinitive (course / qualification / skill) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Conjugate (course / qualification / skill) to match the subject and the time clues in the sentence.

items.sentence: I'm doing an online English ...... (course / qualification / skill).

explanationContent.whyCorrect: An online English course is something you study on.

wrongOptions.course / qualification / skill: The bare infinitive (course / qualification / skill) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Conjugate (course / qualification / skill) to match the subject and the time clues in the sentence.

items.sentence: This certificate is a useful ...... (course / qualification / skill) for teachers.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: A certificate is a teaching qualification.

wrongOptions.course / qualification / skill: The bare infinitive (course / qualification / skill) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Time words like already, yet, since, and for are strong signals for present perfect.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Conjugate (course / qualification / skill) to match the subject and the time clues in the sentence.

items.sentence: You have ...... (make progress / make sure / take an exam) a lot of ...... (make progress / make sure / take an exam) since September.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Collocation: made a lot of progress since September.

wrongOptions.make progress / make sure / take an exam: The bare infinitive (make progress / make sure / take an exam) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple misses the link to now — present perfect needs have/has + past participle.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Time words like already, yet, since, and for are strong signals for present perfect.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect (have/has + past participle) links past actions to now or unfinished time.

items.sentence: I'm going to ...... (make progress / make sure / take an exam) in biology next week.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: You take an exam in a subject — you don't "make" one.

wrongOptions.make progress / make sure / take an exam: The bare infinitive (make progress / make sure / take an exam) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Choose the future form that matches whether the sentence predicts, plans, or arranges.

items.sentence: Please ...... (make progress / make sure / take an exam) your name is on the paper.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Make sure your name is on the paper = check carefully.

wrongOptions.make progress / make sure / take an exam: The bare infinitive (make progress / make sure / take an exam) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Conjugate (make progress / make sure / take an exam) to match the subject and the time clues in the sentence.

items.sentenceAfter: if we'll get our marks today.
items.originalSentence: I **search / wonder** if we'll get our marks today.
items.completedSentence: I wonder if we'll get our marks today.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "wonder" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "wonder" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "search" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.search: "search" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("if we'll get our marks today.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.originalSentence: The new school **mark / term** starts in September.
items.completedSentence: The new school term starts in September.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "term" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "term" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "mark" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.mark: "mark" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("starts in September.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.sentenceAfter: me to bring my dictionary tomorrow?
items.originalSentence: Can you **remind / revise** me to bring my dictionary tomorrow?
items.completedSentence: Can you remind me to bring my dictionary tomorrow?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "remind" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "remind" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "revise" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.revise: "revise" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("me to bring my dictionary tomorrow?").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.sentenceBefore: Ask Eva for help. She's a science
items.originalSentence: Ask Eva for help. She's a science **expert / brain**.
items.completedSentence: Ask Eva for help. She's a science expert .

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "expert" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "expert" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "brain" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.brain: "brain" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence (".").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.sentenceAfter: artist and his drawings are amazing.
items.originalSentence: Daniel is a **smart / talented** artist and his drawings are amazing.
items.completedSentence: Daniel is a talented artist and his drawings are amazing.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "talented" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "talented" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "smart" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.smart: "smart" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("artist and his drawings are amazing.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.originalSentence: Amy is very **clever / mental** and learns things quickly.
items.completedSentence: Amy is very clever and learns things quickly.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "clever" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "clever" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "mental" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.mental: "mental" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("and learns things quickly.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.sentenceBefore: I didn't know the answer, so I
items.originalSentence: I didn't know the answer, so I **hesitated / guessed**.
items.completedSentence: I didn't know the answer, so I guessed .

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "guessed" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "guessed" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "hesitated" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.hesitated: "hesitated" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence (".").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.originalSentence: Have you ever **considered / concentrated** studying abroad?
items.completedSentence: Have you ever considered studying abroad?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "considered" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "considered" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "concentrated" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.concentrated: "concentrated" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("studying abroad?").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.originalSentence: We wrote a **subject / report** about our history project.
items.completedSentence: We wrote a report about our history project.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "report" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "report" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "subject" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.subject: "subject" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("about our history project.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

exercises.title: Crossword
exercises.instructions: Complete the crossword. All the answers are words in bold in exercise B.
exercises.studentInstruction: Solve the crossword. The answers are the bold words from the previous exercise.

items.clue: You should ...... your notes before the test. (6)

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Revise = study your notes again before a test.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "revise" (6 letters) matches the definition: You should ___ your notes before the test.

wrongOptions.smart: "smart" is a different word from the crossword — check the clue definition again.
wrongOptions.subject: "subject" is a different word from the crossword — check the clue definition again.
wrongOptions.mental: "mental" is a different word from the crossword — check the clue definition again.
wrongOptions.search: "search" is a different word from the crossword — check the clue definition again.
wrongOptions.brain: "brain" is a different word from the crossword — check the clue definition again.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Count the letters in the clue (6) and spell "revise" carefully.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the clue again — the definition points to one word with a fixed letter count.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Smart is another word for intelligent.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "smart" (5 letters) matches the definition: Another word for intelligent is ___ .

wrongOptions.revise: "revise" is a different word from the crossword — check the clue definition again.
wrongOptions.mental: "mental" is a different word from the crossword — check the clue definition again.
wrongOptions.mark: "mark" is a different word from the crossword — check the clue definition again.
wrongOptions.search: "search" is a different word from the crossword — check the clue definition again.
wrongOptions.brain: "brain" is a different word from the crossword — check the clue definition again.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Count the letters in the clue (5) and spell "smart" carefully.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Synonym clues need the exact word with the right number of letters — not a similar phrase.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Maths is a school subject you study.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "subject" (7 letters) matches the definition: Maths is my favourite school ___ .

wrongOptions.revise: "revise" is a different word from the crossword — check the clue definition again.
wrongOptions.mental: "mental" is a different word from the crossword — check the clue definition again.
wrongOptions.search: "search" is a different word from the crossword — check the clue definition again.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Count the letters in the clue (7) and spell "subject" carefully.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the clue again — the definition points to one word with a fixed letter count.

items.clue: The room was noisy, so I couldn't ...... . (11)

explanationContent.whyCorrect: When it's noisy, you can't concentrate.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "concentrate" (11 letters) matches the definition: The room was noisy, so I couldn't ___ .
explanationContent.commonMistake: Double-check long words like "concentrate" — it has 11 letters.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the clue again — the definition points to one word with a fixed letter count.

items.clue: Chess is good exercise for your ...... abilities. (6)

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Chess trains your mental (thinking) abilities.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "mental" (6 letters) matches the definition: Chess is good exercise for your ___ abilities.

wrongOptions.revise: "revise" is a different word from the crossword — check the clue definition again.
wrongOptions.smart: "smart" is a different word from the crossword — check the clue definition again.
wrongOptions.subject: "subject" is a different word from the crossword — check the clue definition again.
wrongOptions.search: "search" is a different word from the crossword — check the clue definition again.
wrongOptions.brain: "brain" is a different word from the crossword — check the clue definition again.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Count the letters in the clue (6) and spell "mental" carefully.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the clue again — the definition points to one word with a fixed letter count.

items.clue: I got a good ...... in my geography test. (4)

explanationContent.whyCorrect: A good mark is a high grade in a test.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "mark" (4 letters) matches the definition: I got a good ___ in my geography test.

wrongOptions.smart: "smart" is a different word from the crossword — check the clue definition again.
wrongOptions.brain: "brain" is a different word from the crossword — check the clue definition again.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Count the letters in the clue (4) and spell "mark" carefully.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the clue again — the definition points to one word with a fixed letter count.

items.clue: I need to ...... for information about this topic. (6)

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Search for information = look for it.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "search" (6 letters) matches the definition: I need to ___ for information about this topic.

wrongOptions.revise: "revise" is a different word from the crossword — check the clue definition again.
wrongOptions.smart: "smart" is a different word from the crossword — check the clue definition again.
wrongOptions.subject: "subject" is a different word from the crossword — check the clue definition again.
wrongOptions.mental: "mental" is a different word from the crossword — check the clue definition again.
wrongOptions.brain: "brain" is a different word from the crossword — check the clue definition again.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Count the letters in the clue (6) and spell "search" carefully.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the clue again — the definition points to one word with a fixed letter count.

items.clue: He ...... before answering the difficult question. (9)

explanationContent.whyCorrect: He hesitated = paused before answering.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "hesitated" (9 letters) matches the definition: He ___ before answering the difficult question.
explanationContent.commonMistake: Double-check long words like "hesitated" — it has 9 letters.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the clue again — the definition points to one word with a fixed letter count.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The human brain controls thinking.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "brain" (5 letters) matches the definition: The human ___ is very powerful.

wrongOptions.revise: "revise" is a different word from the crossword — check the clue definition again.
wrongOptions.smart: "smart" is a different word from the crossword — check the clue definition again.
wrongOptions.mental: "mental" is a different word from the crossword — check the clue definition again.
wrongOptions.mark: "mark" is a different word from the crossword — check the clue definition again.

exercises.title: Phrasal verbs (diary entry)
exercises.instructions: Write one word in each gap, one gap at a time.
exercises.studentInstruction: Write one word in each gap, one gap at a time.
exercises.passage: We had a history test today. First, Mr Brown told us to turn our papers (1) ...... and write our names. Then he read (2) ...... the questions so everyone could hear them. We couldn't look (3) ...... any dates in our books, of course. I made a mistake in the first answer, so I rubbed it (4) ...... and wrote it again. Later, I crossed (5) ...... a whole sentence because it was wrong. I nearly ripped (6) ...... my paper because I was so nervous! Before the test ended, I wrote (7) ...... three important names I had remembered. At the end, Mr Brown pointed (8) ...... that we should check our spelling.

gapExplanationContent.whyCorrect: Turn exam papers over to start writing on the back.
gapExplanationContent.commonMistake: Read the whole line in the passage — tense, preposition, or word form must match the surrounding context.
gapExplanationContent.usefulTip: Read the sentence before and after the gap — the story context usually signals tense and word choice.
gapExplanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose who/which/that to match the antecedent.

gapExplanationContent.whyCorrect: Read the questions out loud for everyone.
gapExplanationContent.commonMistake: Read the whole line in the passage — tense, preposition, or word form must match the surrounding context.
gapExplanationContent.usefulTip: Duration phrases (all day, since, for) often signal present perfect or continuous forms.
gapExplanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose who/which/that to match the antecedent.

gapExplanationContent.whyCorrect: Look up dates = find them in a book (not look at the ceiling).
gapExplanationContent.commonMistake: Read the whole line in the passage — tense, preposition, or word form must match the surrounding context.
gapExplanationContent.usefulTip: Read the sentence before and after the gap — the story context usually signals tense and word choice.
gapExplanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose who/which/that to match the antecedent.

gapExplanationContent.whyCorrect: Rub a mistake out with an eraser.
gapExplanationContent.commonMistake: Read the whole line in the passage — tense, preposition, or word form must match the surrounding context.
gapExplanationContent.usefulTip: Read the sentence before and after the gap — the story context usually signals tense and word choice.
gapExplanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose who/which/that to match the antecedent.

gapExplanationContent.commonMistake: Read the whole line in the passage — tense, preposition, or word form must match the surrounding context.
gapExplanationContent.usefulTip: Read the sentence before and after the gap — the story context usually signals tense and word choice.
gapExplanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose who/which/that to match the antecedent.

gapExplanationContent.whyCorrect: Rip the paper up when nervous.
gapExplanationContent.commonMistake: Read the whole line in the passage — tense, preposition, or word form must match the surrounding context.
gapExplanationContent.usefulTip: Read the sentence before and after the gap — the story context usually signals tense and word choice.
gapExplanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose who/which/that to match the antecedent.

gapExplanationContent.commonMistake: Read the whole line in the passage — tense, preposition, or word form must match the surrounding context.
gapExplanationContent.usefulTip: Read the sentence before and after the gap — the story context usually signals tense and word choice.
gapExplanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose who/which/that to match the antecedent.

gapExplanationContent.whyCorrect: Point out = tell/remind everyone to check spelling.
gapExplanationContent.commonMistake: Read the whole line in the passage — tense, preposition, or word form must match the surrounding context.
gapExplanationContent.usefulTip: Read the sentence before and after the gap — the story context usually signals tense and word choice.
gapExplanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose who/which/that to match the antecedent.

exercises.title: Prepositional phrases
exercises.instructions: Each of the words in bold is in the wrong sentence. Write the correct word.
exercises.studentInstruction: Each of the words in bold is in the wrong sentence. Write the correct word.

items.sentence: I learnt all the irregular verbs by fact.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: By heart = from memory; by fact is not a phrase.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: The highlighted word breaks the collocation or meaning — choose the word that fits naturally in this context.

wrongOptions.fact: "fact" is the highlighted error — use "heart" here instead.
wrongOptions.a: "a" does not fit this slot — the correct word is "heart".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Do not leave "fact" — the rule here needs "heart" in the highlighted slot.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the sentence with your correction inserted — if it still sounds wrong, you may have changed the wrong word.

items.sentence: Are you in instance of more homework at weekends?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: In favour of = supporting an idea; in instance of is wrong.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: The highlighted word breaks the collocation or meaning — choose the word that fits naturally in this context.

wrongOptions.instance: "instance" is the highlighted error — use "favour" here instead.

explanationContent.commonMistake: The error is in "instance", not elsewhere — replace it with "favour" and read the full sentence to check it sounds natural.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the sentence with your correction inserted — if it still sounds wrong, you may have changed the wrong word.

items.sentence: The lesson looked difficult, but in heart it was quite simple.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: In fact = actually/truthfully; in heart is not used here.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: The highlighted word breaks the collocation or meaning — choose the word that fits naturally in this context.

wrongOptions.heart: "heart" is the highlighted error — use "fact" here instead.
wrongOptions.used: "used" does not fit this slot — the correct word is "fact".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Do not leave "heart" — the rule here needs "fact" in the highlighted slot.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the sentence with your correction inserted — if it still sounds wrong, you may have changed the wrong word.

items.sentence: Some subjects, for conclusion history, require a lot of reading.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: For instance = for example; for conclusion is wrong.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: The highlighted word breaks the collocation or meaning — choose the word that fits naturally in this context.

wrongOptions.conclusion: "conclusion" is the highlighted error — use "instance" here instead.

explanationContent.commonMistake: The error is in "conclusion", not elsewhere — replace it with "instance" and read the full sentence to check it sounds natural.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the sentence with your correction inserted — if it still sounds wrong, you may have changed the wrong word.

items.sentence: In favour, students at this school work very hard.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: In general = usually/on the whole; In favour needs "of".
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

wrongOptions.favour: "favour" is the highlighted error — use "general" here instead.

explanationContent.commonMistake: The error is in "favour", not elsewhere — replace it with "general" and read the full sentence to check it sounds natural.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the sentence with your correction inserted — if it still sounds wrong, you may have changed the wrong word.

items.sentence: You can finish your essay with the phrase 'In general'.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Essays often end with In conclusion; In general comes earlier.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: The highlighted word breaks the collocation or meaning — choose the word that fits naturally in this context.

wrongOptions.general: "general" is the highlighted error — use "conclusion" here instead.

explanationContent.commonMistake: The error is in "general", not elsewhere — replace it with "conclusion" and read the full sentence to check it sounds natural.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the sentence with your correction inserted — if it still sounds wrong, you may have changed the wrong word.

exercises.title: Word formation
exercises.instructions: Complete by changing the form of the word in capitals.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete by changing the form of the word in capitals.

items.sentence: Good ...... is important for every child. **EDUCATE**

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is noun from EDUCATE, so the correct answer is "education".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple is wrong when the sentence links past to now (for, since, already, yet, ever).
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links a past action to the present or unfinished time.

items.sentence: This course is easy because it's for ...... . **BEGIN**

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is plural noun for people starting, so the correct answer is "beginners".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

exercises.title: Word patterns
exercises.instructions: Write one word in each gap.
exercises.studentInstruction: Write one word in each gap.

items.sentence: Nobody should cheat ...... a test.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: We cheat in a test (inside an exam).
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the full sentence with your answer inserted — if it sounds wrong, check tense, preposition, or word form.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: We're learning ...... the human brain in science.

exercises.title: Word patterns (sentence transformation)
exercises.instructions: Complete each second sentence using the word given, so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence. Write between two and five words.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete each second sentence using the word given, so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence. Write between two and five words.

items.promptSentence: Mia is very good at drawing portraits.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is good at, so the correct transformation is "talented at drawing".
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Verb + preposition patterns (succeed in, capable of, talented at) are fixed — learn them as chunks.
explanationContent.wordCountMistake: Write between 2 and 5 words, including the keyword "TALENTED" unchanged. Contractions such as don't count as one word.

wrongOptions.talented: Do not leave out or alter the keyword "TALENTED" — it must appear in your answer.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check that your answer keeps the keyword, matches the word limit, and preserves the meaning of the prompt sentence.
explanationContent.similarExample: Similar pattern: keep "TALENTED" and rewrite the grammar to match the prompt meaning.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Count your words (2–5) before you check — include "TALENTED" unchanged and read the completed second sentence aloud.

items.promptSentence: The students were tired but didn't stop working.
items.targetSentence: The students were tired but they ...... their work.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is didn't stop working, so the correct transformation is "continued with".
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Passive voice uses be + past participle — keep the keyword and change the structure around it.
explanationContent.wordCountMistake: Write between 2 and 5 words, including the keyword "CONTINUED" unchanged. Contractions such as don't count as one word.

wrongOptions.continued: Do not leave out or alter the keyword "CONTINUED" — it must appear in your answer.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check that your answer keeps the keyword, matches the word limit, and preserves the meaning of the prompt sentence.
explanationContent.similarExample: Similar pattern: keep "CONTINUED" and rewrite the grammar to match the prompt meaning.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Count your words (2–5) before you check — include "CONTINUED" unchanged and read the completed second sentence aloud.

items.promptSentence: I have no information about this subject.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is no information about, so the correct transformation is "don't know anything about".
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Use "KNOW" exactly as given and change the grammar around it to preserve the meaning of the first sentence.
explanationContent.wordCountMistake: Write between 2 and 5 words, including the keyword "KNOW" unchanged. Contractions such as don't count as one word.

wrongOptions.know: Do not leave out or alter the keyword "KNOW" — it must appear in your answer.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check that your answer keeps the keyword, matches the word limit, and preserves the meaning of the prompt sentence.
explanationContent.similarExample: Similar pattern: keep "KNOW" and rewrite the grammar to match the prompt meaning.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Count your words (2–5) before you check — include "KNOW" unchanged and read the completed second sentence aloud.

items.promptSentence: Lucy couldn't understand the exercise, so Tom helped her.

explanationContent.grammarFocus: Use "HELPING" exactly as given and change the grammar around it to preserve the meaning of the first sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check that your answer keeps the keyword, matches the word limit, and preserves the meaning of the prompt sentence.
explanationContent.similarExample: Similar pattern: keep "HELPING" and rewrite the grammar to match the prompt meaning.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Count your words (2–5) before you check — include "HELPING" unchanged and read the completed second sentence aloud.

items.promptSentence: Nobody can finish this project in ten minutes.
items.targetSentence: Nobody ...... this project in ten minutes.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is nobody can, so the correct transformation is "is capable of finishing".
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Verb + preposition patterns (succeed in, capable of, talented at) are fixed — learn them as chunks.
explanationContent.wordCountMistake: Write between 2 and 5 words, including the keyword "CAPABLE" unchanged. Contractions such as don't count as one word.

explanationContent.similarExample: Similar: He is capable of solving difficult problems.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Count your words (2–5) before you check — include "CAPABLE" unchanged and read the completed second sentence aloud.
